# Pull Request Title
<!-- Use: [TYPE] Short description — e.g. [feat] Add X feature, [fix] Resolve Y bug -->
<!-- Keep title concise and prefixed with a type (feat, fix, docs, chore, refactor, perf, test, ci) -->

## Description
<!--
  A short description of what this PR changes and why.
  Summarize the intent and the high-level approach.
-->
- Summary:
- Motivation / Context:

## Related Issue(s)
- Fixes: # (issue number) or N/A
- Relates to: # (issue number) or N/A

## Type of change
- [ ] feat (new feature)
- [ ] fix (bug fix)
- [ ] docs (documentation only)
- [ ] style (formatting, no code change)
- [ ] refactor (code change that neither fixes a bug nor adds a feature)
- [ ] perf (improves performance)
- [ ] test (adds or updates tests)
- [ ] chore (build process or auxiliary tools)
- [ ] ci (CI configuration)

## Checklist (required)
- [ ] I have read the contributing guidelines.
- [ ] This PR has a clear description and title.
- [ ] My code follows the repository style and lint rules.
- [ ] I ran the test suite locally and all tests pass.
- [ ] New and existing unit/integration tests are added/updated as needed.
- [ ] Relevant documentation is updated (README, docs, inline comments).
- [ ] I updated/added migration scripts if the change requires it.
- [ ] This change has no sensitive information or secrets.

Tips:
- Keep PRs small and focused — prefer multiple small PRs to one large one.
- Use explicit commit messages and reference the issue numbers.
- Update docs and tests together with implementation changes.
